direction: Reframe the undergraduate 'Limited Descent' project (Math 693A) as a well-posed constrained path-finding problem and ask: does the original ad-hoc 'rotate the travel direction clockwise/counter-clockwise until the next step is walkable' heuristic actually approach the constrained shortest safe descent path, or merely a feasible-but-arbitrary one? The original method takes fixed-step steepest descent over an analytic terrain f(x,y) and, whenever a step would drop more steeply than a maximum grade theta (5 degrees), rotates the direction in 1-degree increments until the drop is <= l*tan(theta). Its own Discussion identified the fatal confound: the clockwise, counter-clockwise, and either-rotation strategies each converge to a DIFFERENT endpoint, so comparing their total path lengths is ill-posed. Fix that confound by giving the objective a single defined target (a sink/basin representing the trailhead or road) so all strategies descend to the same point and path lengths become comparable. Then compare three schemes - (a) the original rotation heuristic, (b) a principled projection of the gradient onto the boundary of the feasible descent cone (equivalently, an exact-theta directional step), and (c) unconstrained fixed-step steepest descent - against a ground-truth optimum computed by a grid shortest-safe-path (Dijkstra/A*) under the SAME grade constraint. Evaluate over several analytic terrain functions and >= 5 initial points (injured-hiker locations) per terrain, reporting: feasibility (max-grade constraint satisfied at EVERY step), total 3-D path length, optimality gap versus the grid optimum, and convergence (iterations to reach the target, with a bounded rotation loop). Report mean +/- SD over initial points and bootstrap 95% confidence intervals for any cross-strategy comparison. The headline output is the optimality gap of the rotation heuristic versus the constrained optimum, and whether the clockwise/counter-clockwise asymmetry is a real effect or an artifact of the original's undefined endpoint - explicitly admitting the likely outcome that the heuristic is feasible but far from optimal, which is a legitimate negative/limitation result.

context.origin: Reworking of 'Limited Descent: The Use of Gradient Descent in Mountain Rescue' by Gianni Pucillo (Math 693A final). Original PDF: C:/Users/gdp/Downloads/Math 693A Final.pdf.

constraints.compute: Pure CPU, NumPy/SciPy only. No GPU, no external data, no network. Every objective is a closed-form analytic function evaluated in-sandbox; a full sweep targets < 15 minutes wall-clock.
constraints.no_external_datasets: Terrains are analytic objective functions (sums of Gaussians / quadratic basins at specified peak and sink locations). DO NOT call the dataset_fetch tool; there is nothing to fetch.

context.ceiling: Workshop track / short methods note (a numerical-optimization reproducibility-and-correction note built on an undergraduate project). Not a flagship-venue result. Per KICKOFF.md Section 1.
hard_rules: feasibility is the primary correctness gate: the produced path must satisfy the descent-grade constraint (drop <= l*tan(theta)) at EVERY step; a path that violates its own constraint anywhere is invalid regardless of length

the rotation loop MUST be bounded by a turn cap (the original appendix code has an unbounded inner while-loop that can spin forever on a cliff face); the outer scheme MUST have a max-iteration budget and report convergence
the objective MUST have a single defined convergence target (sink) so all strategies descend to the same endpoint; path lengths are only compared across strategies that share that target
validate every analytic gradient against finite differences (or use autodiff) before trusting any descent result
>= 5 initial points per terrain with mean +/- SD reported, and bootstrap 95% confidence intervals for any cross-strategy comparison
reproduce a reference optimum (grid shortest-safe-path under the same grade constraint) before making ANY optimality / 'shorter path' claim, and report the optimality gap, not just raw path length
flag the clockwise-vs-counter-clockwise asymmetry as a counterintuitive finding requiring interpretation: decide whether it is a real effect or an artifact of the original's undefined endpoint
report path-length and feasibility sensitivity to the discretization choices (step length 0.1, rotation increment 1 degree) before claiming a 'near-continuous' path
